content.text: What is Model Selection for Latency?
In agentic AI systems, every invocation of a language model contributes directly to end-to-end latency.

is the practice of choosing — and dynamically routing between — language models of different sizes and capability levels so that each task is completed within its latency budget at the lowest possible cost. Rather than using one flagship model for everything, you build a tiered dispatch layer that matches task complexity to the appropriate model tier.

The core insight is that most tasks in an agent pipeline are not equal in difficulty. Classifying a user query as 'billing' or 'technical', extracting a date from a sentence, or checking whether a tool call result is valid requires little reasoning capacity. Running a powerful reasoning model for these tasks wastes compute and adds hundreds of milliseconds per hop. Reserving powerful models for tasks that genuinely need them — multi-step planning, code generation, complex analysis — keeps the overall pipeline fast and economical.

Agentic pipelines chain many model calls in sequence. A research agent might call a model to plan, another to search, several to summarise sources, and one more to synthesise results. If each call takes 5–10 seconds on a large model, the aggregate latency for a 10-step pipeline reaches 50–100 seconds — unacceptable for interactive use.

Unlike single-turn chat, agents also run tool-augmented loops where the model decides whether to call another tool, making latency additive across every loop iteration. Optimising the model choice at each decision point is therefore the highest-leverage lever for overall pipeline speed, above prompt compression or infrastructure tuning.
fast models complete in 200–800 ms, immediately improving user-perceived responsiveness for the majority of requests.
small models cost 20–60× less per token than frontier models; routing simple tasks to them cuts API spend without sacrificing quality.
fast models have shorter queue times and higher concurrency limits, allowing more parallel agent branches.
complex tasks still reach the most capable model, so accuracy does not degrade on the workloads that need it.
when a large model is rate-limited or unavailable, the routing layer can fall back to a capable mid-tier model rather than failing.
a classifier that routes tasks incorrectly sends complex queries to weak models (wrong answers) or simple queries to large models (wasted spend). The router itself needs to be accurate and fast.
even fast models have a minimum latency of ~200 ms for short prompts. For tasks that must complete in <100 ms, semantic caching or pre-computation is necessary — no model call will suffice.
small models fail qualitatively on tasks above their capability ceiling. You must benchmark task types on each tier to know where these cliffs are before routing in production.
small models typically have smaller context windows. A task that fits in the context of a large model may need to be chunked when routed to a small model.
validating that a small model's output is good enough before using it adds latency if you run a second model call for verification.
Model selection for latency is built around three mechanisms that can be combined:
task type is known ahead of time (e.g., 'this step always does named-entity extraction') and the model is hard-coded in the pipeline configuration.
a lightweight model or rule-based classifier inspects the incoming prompt and routes it to a tier. The classifier runs in <100 ms and determines which tier handles the request.

route every request to a fast model first. If the model's output confidence score (or a post-hoc verifier) falls below a threshold, escalate the request to the next tier. Only requests that genuinely need it pay the cost of a larger model.
A typical tiered architecture uses three model classes. Tier 1 (fast) models like
handle classification, extraction, and validation with 200–800 ms latency. Tier 2 (balanced) models like
handle reasoning, tool selection, and drafting with 1–4 s latency. Tier 3 (powerful) models like
handle complex synthesis, deep code generation, and long-form reasoning with 5–30 s latency.

attrs.alt: Model selection for latency: decision pipeline routing tasks through three tiers based on complexity, with confidence cascade and semantic caching

content.text: any agent that responds to a user in real time and has a perceived latency SLA (e.g., < 5 s for first token) benefits from model selection routing.
batch processing or document-level pipelines where cost per item matters more than latency still benefit from routing simple sub-tasks (e.g., classification, validation) to small models.

when an agent decides to call tools in a loop, routing the 'which tool should I call next?' step to a fast model and the 'synthesise findings' step to a powerful model halves the number of slow model calls.
a shared agent serving many different request types (customer support, code generation, data extraction) cannot assume a uniform difficulty level; dynamic routing adapts automatically.

You do not need model selection routing when all tasks are uniformly complex, when the pipeline has a single step, or when latency constraints are loose enough that a single model tier satisfies all requirements.

Audit your pipeline. List every model call and categorise each by its output type: classification, extraction, generation, reasoning, verification. Classify each as complexity-low, -medium, or -high based on the output quality needed.
Benchmark candidate models per task category. Run 50–100 representative examples through each tier and measure accuracy, latency (p50 and p99), and token count. Document where each tier fails.

Build or select a routing layer. For rule-based routing, use prompt length, keyword presence, or task type tags. For ML routing, fine-tune a small classifier or use a fast model (Tier 1) to classify each request with a structured output schema.

Implement a model registry. Store model ID, tier, context window, cost-per-token, and per-task accuracy benchmarks in a configuration object. The routing layer queries this registry rather than hard-coding model names.

Add confidence cascading for uncertain cases. After a Tier 1 response, parse a confidence field (e.g., include it in the structured output schema) or run a lightweight verifier. Escalate to Tier 2 if confidence is below threshold.
Enable streaming for all tiers. Even slow models feel faster when the first tokens arrive within 500 ms. Streaming is especially valuable for Tier 3 models where total generation time exceeds 10 s.
Instrument every routing decision. Log which tier handled each request, latency, token count, and whether escalation occurred. This data drives ongoing tier threshold tuning.
Add semantic caching upstream. Cache responses for semantically similar queries before any model call. A cache hit removes the latency of all tiers.

import anthropic
import json
from dataclasses import dataclass

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

@dataclass
class TieredResponse:
    answer: str
    confidence: float  # 0.0 – 1.0
    model_used: str
    escalated: bool


def call_with_confidence(prompt: str, model: str, max_tokens: int = 256) -> TieredResponse:
    """Ask the model to include a confidence score in structured output."""
    system = (
        "Answer the user question. Reply with JSON:\n"
        "{\"answer\": \"...\", \"confidence\": 0.0-1.0}\n"
        "confidence=1.0 means certain, 0.0 means a guess."
    )
    response = client.messages.create(
        model=model,
        max_tokens=max_tokens,
        system=system,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    data = json.loads(response.content[0].text)
    return TieredResponse(
        answer=data["answer"],
        confidence=data["confidence"],
        model_used=model,
        escalated=False,
    )


def cascading_call(prompt: str, threshold: float = 0.75) -> TieredResponse:
    """
    Try Tier 1 first. Escalate to Tier 2 if confidence is too low.
    Escalate to Tier 3 if Tier 2 is also uncertain.
    """
    tiers = [
        ("claude-haiku-3-5",  256),
        ("claude-sonnet-4",   512),
        ("claude-opus-4",    1024),
    ]

    result = None
    for model, max_tokens in tiers:
        result = call_with_confidence(prompt, model, max_tokens)
        if result.confidence >= threshold:
            return result
        # Mark escalation and try the next tier
        result.escalated = True

    # Return best available answer even if confidence never crossed threshold
    return result


if __name__ == "__main__":
    question = "What does the 'idempotent' property mean in the context of HTTP PUT requests?"
    result = cascading_call(question, threshold=0.80)
    print(f"Model: {result.model_used}  Confidence: {result.confidence:.2f}  Escalated: {result.escalated}")
    print(result.answer)

Consider a customer-support agent that handles billing questions, technical troubleshooting, and general product queries. Without model selection, every message — including trivial greetings — hits a frontier model at full cost and latency. With a tiered pipeline:
('What are your business hours?') is classified as complexity-low and answered by a fast model from a pre-computed answer library.
('I was charged twice') requires account-context retrieval and empathetic drafting — routed to the balanced tier.
involving logs, stack traces, and multi-step diagnosis is routed to the powerful tier.

The routing classifier itself runs on the fast model, adding only ~300 ms. The result is that ~70% of requests (FAQ / simple) complete in under 1 second, ~25% (billing / support) in 2–4 seconds, and ~5% (complex technical) in 8–15 seconds — whereas a single-model approach would put every request in the 4–8 s range.

import anthropic
import json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

FAST    = "claude-haiku-3-5"
BALANCED = "claude-sonnet-4"
POWERFUL = "claude-opus-4"

ROUTER_SYSTEM = """
You are a routing classifier for a customer support agent.
Given the user message, output JSON:
  {"complexity": "low" | "medium" | "high", "topic": str}

low    = FAQ, greetings, simple yes/no
medium = account questions, billing, standard troubleshooting
high   = multi-step technical diagnosis, complex integrations, escalations
"""


def route(message: str) -> tuple[str, str]:
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model=FAST,
        max_tokens=80,
        system=ROUTER_SYSTEM,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": message}],
    )
    data = json.loads(resp.content[0].text)
    complexity = data["complexity"]
    model = {
        "low": FAST,
        "medium": BALANCED,
        "high": POWERFUL,
    }[complexity]
    return model, complexity


def answer(message: str, model: str) -> str:
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model=model,
        max_tokens=512,
        system="You are a helpful customer support agent.",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": message}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text


def handle(message: str) -> dict:
    model, complexity = route(message)
    reply = answer(message, model)
    return {"reply": reply, "complexity": complexity, "model": model}


# Example usage
for msg in [
    "What are your opening hours?",
    "I was charged twice on my last invoice.",
    "My webhook integration fails intermittently with a 502. Here are my logs: ...",
]:
    result = handle(msg)
    print(f"[{result['complexity'].upper()}] -> {result['model']}")
    print(result["reply"][:120], "...\n")
Using a heavy model as the router:

if the router that decides which tier to use itself runs on a large model, you pay the large-model latency for every request — negating the benefit. The router must run on the fastest tier or use rule-based logic.

a long prompt is not necessarily complex. A 2 000-token document extraction prompt is simple; a 100-token adversarial reasoning question is hard. Use output quality benchmarks, not input length, to calibrate tier boundaries.

assigning tasks to tiers based on intuition without measuring actual accuracy on representative examples leads to capability cliffs in production. Always validate small-model performance before routing to it.
routing to a smaller model but displaying results only after full generation wastes the latency advantage. Enable streaming and display tokens as they arrive.
model IDs change with provider releases. Hardcoding
strings across dozens of pipeline steps means a model upgrade requires hunting down every reference. Centralise model IDs in a registry.
without per-request logs of which tier was used, you cannot measure real-world tier distribution or diagnose routing errors.
Define latency SLAs per task type
before choosing tiers. Work backwards from the UX requirement (e.g., 'first token within 500 ms for interactive steps') to the maximum model size you can afford.
as a single configuration object. Include model ID, tier, context window, input/output price per million tokens, and per-task accuracy from your benchmarks.
using a vector similarity threshold (e.g., cosine > 0.97) to serve repeated or near-duplicate requests without any model call.
latency. A fast model that occasionally spikes to 4 s under load may violate your SLA for 1% of requests. Monitor tail latency per tier.
when provider models are updated. A model ID that pointed to a capable model six months ago may now point to a different checkpoint with different performance characteristics.
Prefer structured output for classifier calls
or a strict JSON system prompt so the routing layer can parse the tier decision without fragile regex.
and fall back gracefully. If a Tier 2 call exceeds 8 s, either fall back to a cached result or escalate to Tier 3 with a shorter max_tokens budget.

The performance impact of model selection routing is multiplicative when an agent runs many tool loops. Consider an agent with 8 sequential model calls, 4 of which are routing/classification and 4 are generation. If routing calls drop from 3 s to 400 ms, total pipeline latency falls by 10.4 s — roughly 50% for a 20-second pipeline.
have the highest concurrency headroom and lowest per-request latency. They scale horizontally with minimal cost increase.

(e.g., Redis + pgvector or a managed vector store) should be sized for the query volume. Cache hit rates of 20–40% are common in production support agents and eliminate model cost entirely for those requests.
is typically stateless and can be replicated trivially. Use connection pooling when calling the Tier 1 classifier to avoid per-request TLS handshake overhead.
compound with model selection: a Tier 1 model at 512 max output tokens costs a fraction of a Tier 3 model at 2 048 tokens. Keep

A well-tuned model selection pipeline reduces average latency by 40–60% and API cost by 60–80% compared with routing all traffic to a single frontier model, with no measurable quality regression when tier boundaries are calibrated correctly.
Swap model IDs without changing client code; built-in routing and fallback
Route LangChain chains or runnables based on classifier output
Route queries to different index types or models per query type
Single endpoint for many models; supports per-request model selection
Log per-tier accuracy and latency; run evals on tier boundary changes
Trace multi-step pipelines; tag spans by model tier for latency analysis
Cache tier-1/tier-2 responses by embedding similarity
claude-* models support streaming for all tiers; reduces perceived latency

an attacker can craft a user message that causes the routing classifier to send the query to a less capable tier where safety guardrails may be weaker. Validate routing decisions and apply safety checks independently of the model tier.
a confidence cascade that never exceeds the threshold will exhaust all tiers. Set a maximum cascade depth and return a safe default response rather than infinitely escalating.

Tier 3 models have stricter rate limits. If your cascade sends too many requests to the powerful tier, you may hit limits and degrade the entire pipeline. Monitor tier distribution and set cascade thresholds conservatively.

if a model is deprecated by the provider but still referenced in your registry as 'Tier 1', calls will fail. Subscribe to provider deprecation notices and treat model IDs as configuration that needs versioned updates.
log which model handled each request for compliance. Some regulated domains require documentation of which AI model produced which output.
Model selection for latency routes each task to the cheapest, fastest model capable of handling it rather than using a single frontier model for everything.

Three-tier architectures (fast / balanced / powerful) cover the full range of agentic tasks: fast models for classification and extraction, balanced for reasoning and tool use, powerful for deep synthesis.
Confidence cascading combines the cost advantages of small models with the quality of large models: start fast, escalate only when needed.
The routing classifier must itself run on the fastest tier; a slow router negates all latency gains downstream.
Semantic caching is complementary and should sit upstream of all model tiers — a cache hit beats even a Tier 1 model call.
Always benchmark candidate models per task category before assigning tier boundaries; capability cliffs vary significantly by domain.
Instrument every routing decision with tier label, model ID, latency, and confidence to enable ongoing threshold calibration.
